summary: My grandparents lived in Matera, a town in Southern Italy perched atop a hill overlooking the Gravina River valley. They resided in the ancient neighborhoods known as i Sassi, where conditions were primitive and poverty was severe. Matera was the starting point of my family's journey, and although it is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site, for us it represents a past of hardship that we had to leave behind.

The Gravina River runs through the valley beneath Matera, where my grandparents lived. Their home overlooked this dry riverbed, which was part of the harsh landscape surrounding the i Sassi neighborhoods. The river and valley framed the difficult environment that shaped their early lives before they emigrated.

I Sassi were the cave neighborhoods built into the mountain side of Matera where my grandparents lived. Thousands of people lived there in unsanitary and primitive conditions, sharing cramped spaces with animals and suffering from diseases like trachoma and malaria. This place was vividly described in Carlo Levi's memoir, capturing the extreme poverty that my family endured before emigrating.

Carlo Levi was the author who famously described the poverty of Matera in his memoir. His work, Christ Stopped at Eboli, gave a vivid and heartbreaking portrayal of the living conditions in i Sassi, which matched what my grandparents experienced. His writing helped bring attention to the plight of people like my family in Southern Italy.

Christ Stopped at Eboli is Carlo Levi’s memoir that describes the harsh realities of life in Southern Italy, particularly in places like Matera and i Sassi. The book’s vivid depiction of poverty, disease, and isolation reflects the environment where my grandparents lived in the early 1900s. It remains a powerful testament to the struggles faced by my family’s generation.

Today, Matera and its ancient cave dwellings, i Sassi, are recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This designation honors the historical and cultural significance of the area, which was once a place of extreme poverty for families like mine. Although it is now quiet and picturesque, for my grandparents it was a place they had to leave behind in search of a better life.

My grandfather, Angelo Raffaele Ambrosecchia, was born and raised in Matera and lived in the Street of Seven Sorrows. He married Paola Rosa there, and together they had their first child in 1900 under very difficult conditions. Angelo emigrated to the United States in 1906, landing in New York and eventually settling in Wethersfield, Connecticut, where he worked at the Comstock Seed Company to support his family.

Paola Rosa Ambrosecchia, my grandmother, was married to Angelo Raffaele in Matera and gave birth to their first child in 1900 at their home on the Street of Seven Sorrows. She endured the harsh living conditions of i Sassi before emigrating to the United States with her husband and children. In Connecticut, she cared for their seven children but never fully integrated into the new community due to language barriers.

The Street of Seven Sorrows was the address in Matera where my grandparents lived in the early 1900s. It was a place marked by hardship and poverty, where my grandfather Angelo and grandmother Paola Rosa raised their first child. This street name seemed to symbolize the suffering endured by families like mine before they emigrated.

Corleto Perticara is the town where my father, Vito Nicola Ambrosecchia, was born in 1904. My grandparents had traveled there during the summers to find work on large plantations, walking 60 miles from Matera. This town represents a transitional place for my family before they emigrated to America.

New York City was the port where my grandfather Angelo Raffaele Ambrosecchia landed on March 30, 1906 after emigrating from Italy. He arrived with very little money but managed to travel from New York to Wethersfield, Connecticut to join his brother. This city was the gateway to a new life for my family in America.

Wethersfield, Connecticut became the new home for my grandparents and their children after emigrating from Italy. Angelo worked at the Comstock Seed Company there, continuing his connection to farm labor. My father grew up attending the local one-room schoolhouse, though his parents remained isolated by language and culture in this small town.

The Comstock Seed Company in Wethersfield, Connecticut was where my grandfather Angelo found work as a farm laborer after emigrating. This job allowed him to continue working the land, much like his ancestors had in Italy. It was a difficult life, but it provided for the family and helped them establish themselves in their new country.

Boston was the city my father moved to as a teenager after growing up in Wethersfield. He left home to seek his own path and eventually changed his name to Nicholas Victor Ambrose. Boston was the first step in his journey away from the family and the immigrant community he had known.

My father, Nicholas Victor Ambrose, served in the United States Army for a period after leaving home. His time in the military was part of his broader journey across the country and beyond, including travels to California, Virginia, Panama, and Argentina. The Army was one of the few structured experiences he had outside his family and immigrant roots.

California was one of the places my father lived after his service in the U.S. Army. He drifted across the country, moving from California to Virginia and beyond, searching for his place. These travels marked his restless spirit and distance from the family home in Connecticut.

Virginia was another stop in my father’s travels after leaving home and serving in the Army. His movement from California to Virginia and then further abroad reflected his unsettled nature and difficulty integrating into communities. These journeys were part of his search for identity.

Panama was one of the international places my father visited during his years of wandering after military service. His travels to Panama and Argentina show how far he roamed in search of a life different from the one he left behind in Connecticut. These experiences were part of his complex story of migration and identity.

Argentina was another distant country my father lived in during his years away from home. His time there, along with Panama and various U.S. states, illustrates his restless search for belonging. Returning from these travels, he eventually settled in Brooklyn with my mother.

Brooklyn became the place where my parents settled after my father’s long travels. My mother, Anna, was born in Brooklyn to immigrant parents from Matera, and she helped my father establish a home there. However, they lived apart from her relatives in Bensonhurst and Williamsburg, raising their family in the poor neighborhood of Weeksville.

John Ambrose was my uncle, born in Wethersfield, Connecticut, and part of our extended family network. He was connected to my mother’s side as well, linking the family between Connecticut and Brooklyn. His presence represents the ties that held our family together across different places.

Bensonhurst, Brooklyn was where my mother’s beloved cousin Giulia lived, representing the Italian immigrant community in New York. Although my parents lived apart from this community, Bensonhurst was part of the larger network of family and cultural ties they maintained. It was a contrast to the isolated life my parents led in Weeksville.

Williamsburg, Brooklyn was another neighborhood where my mother’s relatives lived. My parents chose to live apart from these familiar communities, which made their life more isolated. Williamsburg represents the immigrant enclaves that my parents did not fully join, highlighting their unique family experience.

Weeksville, Brooklyn was the neighborhood where my parents raised their five children. It was a dilapidated, wood-frame area far from the Italian American communities of Bensonhurst and Williamsburg. Living there among strangers and in poverty, my parents repeated the silent, isolated struggle of their own parents in Italy and Connecticut.
